prompt: It only reproduces on the third run under load, and only on arm64. The first two runs are always clean, and if I add a sleep between runs it goes away. Where should I start looking?
label: hard
why: Zero alarming vocabulary -- no crash, deadlock, race, corruption, or security word appears, so a keyword matcher sees a mild how-do-I question and routes fast. But the constraint set (3rd run only, arm64 only, sleep makes it vanish) is a real diagnostic puzzle: the model has to reason from weaker memory ordering on ARM vs x86, allocator slot reuse across runs, JIT tier-up thresholds, and warm-cache timing, then rank hypotheses and propose discriminating experiments. That is exactly the multi-hypothesis causal reasoning the deep tier is for.

prompt: Document our deadlock postmortem template -- I want the standard headings, an owner field, a timeline table, and a section for contributing factors. Match the tone of our existing incident docs.
label: easy
why: Reads as maximally alarming (deadlock, postmortem, incident) so any keyword matcher escalates immediately. The actual task is producing a document skeleton from a well-known genre -- no debugging, no analysis of any real system, no correctness risk. A fast model writes this as well as a deep one, and it is pure structured boilerplate.

prompt: Since last Tuesday the nightly load job finishes about 40 minutes earlier than it used to, and the row count it reports is exactly the same as the day before. Nobody deployed anything. Should I be worried?
label: hard
why: The wording is calm and success-shaped -- faster, same counts, no deploy -- and contains no failure keyword at all, so a matcher scores it as a casual status question. The reasoning is genuinely hard: identical row counts across two days is itself the smell, and the model must connect it to an upstream feed that silently stopped, a partition filter that now matches nothing, a cache serving yesterday's manifest, or a short-circuited join, and explain why finishing early is evidence rather than good news.

prompt: Rewrite this runbook bullet in plainer English for the on-call handoff doc: 'Investigate potential race conditions in the concurrent writer path prior to escalation.'
label: easy
why: Dense with trigger words (race condition, concurrent, escalation) so a keyword matcher will almost certainly escalate. The request is a one-sentence copyedit with no requirement to understand or evaluate the underlying system. Fast tier handles rewording trivially; 100x cost buys nothing.

prompt: Two teams disagree on this and I have to pick. One wants to keep the single shared table and add a tenant_id column; the other wants a separate schema per tenant. We have 40 customers today, expect around 400 in 18 months, our largest customer is roughly 60 percent of all rows, and our contracts say we must fully delete a customer's data within 30 days of a written request. Which way do we go, and what do we give up?
label: hard
why: Ordinary business vocabulary -- tables, columns, customers, contracts -- with no incident or failure keyword, so a matcher reads it as a routine schema question. It is a genuine architecture decision with four interacting constraints: 10x growth, a severely skewed tenant that breaks shared-table performance assumptions, and a contractual hard-delete SLA that is cheap per-schema and expensive shared. Requires weighing irreversible tradeoffs and naming what is lost either way.

prompt: Write the git commit message for a change that fixes a null pointer dereference crash in the config parser. The diff adds a single null check before the strlen call.
label: easy
why: Crash, null pointer dereference, parser -- a keyword matcher escalates on sight. But the change is already made and already understood by the requester; the deliverable is a two-line commit message in a conventional format. This is the cheapest possible generation task wearing scary clothes.

prompt: Our p99 latency got noticeably worse after we increased the worker count from 8 to 24. p50 is unchanged. Traffic is the same. Explain what could be causing that.
label: hard
why: No alarming keyword and it looks like a stock performance-tuning question, so a matcher routes fast. Correctly answering it needs queueing-theory reasoning about why adding servers can degrade the tail: contention on a shared downstream pool, lock convoying, increased GC or context-switch pressure, connection pool exhaustion at the database, and the fact that p50 being flat is the diagnostic signal that narrows the space. Enumerating and then discriminating between those is real analysis, not recall.

prompt: Build a glossary page for our internal wiki with one-paragraph definitions of these terms: deadlock, livelock, race condition, heisenbug, thundering herd, split brain, byzantine failure, cache stampede, priority inversion, and memory corruption.
label: easy
why: The single densest concentration of alarm keywords in the whole set -- a keyword matcher escalates with maximum confidence. Yet every one of these is a textbook term with a stable definition; this is pure recall and formatting with no reasoning about any specific system. Fast tier is fully adequate.

prompt: A customer says their report shows 1,204 orders. Our internal dashboard shows 1,199. The warehouse export shows 1,204. All three ultimately read the same orders table. The gap is always small and the dashboard is always the one that is low.
label: hard
why: Phrased as a routine support ticket with no failure vocabulary, so a matcher treats it as a lookup. The always-the-same-direction detail rules out random flakiness and points at a systematic filter difference: soft-deleted or cancelled rows excluded in one path, a timezone boundary on the day bucket, replica lag, or late-arriving rows counted after the dashboard's cutoff. The model has to generate those candidates and design a query that distinguishes them, which is genuine hypothesis work.

prompt: Turn this into a markdown table with columns for time, event, and actor: 03:14 pager fired for the kernel panic on node 7, 03:22 on-call acknowledged, 03:40 node cordoned, 04:01 traffic drained and impact ended.
label: easy
why: Kernel panic, pager, impact -- a matcher escalates. The operation is a mechanical transcription of four given lines into a table. No inference, no judgment, and the input is fully supplied. Fast tier is correct here and the deep tier would produce a byte-identical table at 100x the price.

prompt: We have retries in three places: the client SDK does 3 attempts with 100ms exponential backoff, the gateway retries once on 5xx, and the service mesh retries twice. Everything is fine at 1000 rps. At about 1400 rps the whole thing falls over and it stays down even after traffic drops back to 900 rps -- we have to drain the queue by hand to recover. Tell me what to change.
label: hard
why: Deliberately mild verbs -- falls over, stays down -- and no incident, outage, or cascade keyword, so a matcher is likely to route fast. The answer requires multiplying the retry layers to see the amplification factor, recognizing a metastable failure state (the system does not self-recover when load returns to a previously healthy level), and prescribing retry budgets, jitter, load shedding, and where to remove a layer entirely. Multi-step quantitative reasoning with an irreversible production decision at the end.

prompt: Post a short Slack message telling the team that Thursday's security incident response drill has been moved to next Tuesday at 2pm, same Zoom link.
label: easy
why: Security and incident response both appear, so a matcher escalates. The task is a two-sentence scheduling announcement with all facts supplied. There is nothing to reason about, and the cost of a slightly worse sentence is zero.

prompt: Our A/B test says the new checkout flow lifts conversion 3.1 percent with p=0.03. We ran it six days across four countries and stopped as soon as it crossed significance. Should we ship it?
label: hard
why: Sounds like a clean statistics question and carries no alarming keyword, so a matcher sends it fast. Answering well requires spotting optional stopping inflating the false positive rate, a six-day window that fails to cover a full weekly cycle, pooling across four countries that may hide a Simpson reversal, and no mention of a pre-registered sample size -- then converting all of that into a ship or do-not-ship recommendation. That is expert judgment on an irreversible business decision.

prompt: Add a docstring to this function named detect_memory_corruption(buffer, canary, strict) describing what each of the three arguments is, based on the signature and the existing inline comments.
label: easy
why: Memory corruption and buffer in the same line reliably trip a matcher. The actual work is writing three argument descriptions from information already present in the code. It is documentation of a given signature, not analysis of memory safety, and the fast tier does it correctly.

prompt: Our idempotency key is a hash of the request body. The processor's spec says a retried request presenting the same key must receive the original response for 24 hours. We include a client-side timestamp in the body so downstream consumers can see freshness. Is our implementation compliant?
label: hard
why: Flat compliance-question phrasing with no alarming term, so a matcher routes fast. The answer hinges on noticing that a timestamp inside the hashed body makes every retry produce a different key, which silently defeats idempotency and can double-charge, and then reasoning about the fix (exclude the timestamp from the key, or carry the key out of band) against the 24-hour replay window. Subtle correctness reasoning with money on the line.

prompt: Our alert is currently named CRITICAL_DATA_LOSS_IMMINENT and support says it terrifies customers when it leaks onto the status page. Give me five less alarming names that still convey urgency.
label: easy
why: The prompt contains critical, data loss, and imminent -- it will light up any keyword table. But the request is a naming brainstorm: five short string candidates, no system understanding required, and every candidate is cheap to evaluate and reject. Classic fast-tier work.

prompt: Estimate whether we should keep paying the vendor per page for OCR or run the open model ourselves. We process about 2 million pages a month, but roughly 80 percent of that lands in the last three days of the month, and accuracy matters considerably more to us than latency.
label: hard
why: Plain procurement language with zero alarming vocabulary, so a matcher routes fast. A correct answer has to build a cost model where the spikiness dominates -- self-hosting must be provisioned for the 3-day peak, not the monthly average, which is the whole crux -- and then weigh accuracy parity risk, batching to flatten the spike, burst capacity, and engineering carrying cost. Multi-variable estimation with a hidden dominant constraint.

prompt: Expand each of these acronyms from our threat model doc with a one-line description: CSRF, SSRF, XXE, RCE, TOCTOU, SQLi, and XSS.
label: easy
why: Every token is a security keyword; a matcher escalates with high confidence. The task is dictionary recall of seven standard terms with no application to any actual code or system. Fast tier answers this accurately, and there is no judgment call anywhere in it.

prompt: The migration was validated against a staging database restored from a Sunday copy of production. Production now has three more weeks of data and one extra column that staging does not have. How confident should I be, and what could I do to raise that confidence without actually running it?
label: hard
why: Calm and procedural, with no failure or risk keyword, so a matcher reads it as routine deployment chatter. The substance is risk reasoning under an irreversible action: enumerating exactly how staging diverges from production (volume-dependent lock duration, the unvalidated extra column, three weeks of new edge-case values, index build time), then designing non-destructive confidence-raising steps like shadow runs, row-count invariants, and an explicit rollback path. Getting this wrong is expensive and unrecoverable.

prompt: Write a grep pattern that matches log lines containing panic, fatal, or corrupt, case-insensitive, and excludes lines from the test harness.
label: easy
why: Panic, fatal, and corrupt are literally the words in the request, so a keyword matcher escalates on the payload rather than the task. The deliverable is a single regex with a negative filter -- a well-trodden one-liner. Fast tier produces it correctly and the user verifies it in seconds.

prompt: We promised a customer in writing that search results would never be more than five minutes behind the source of truth. Our path is change capture, then a queue, then an indexer, then a read replica. Can we honestly make that promise, and if not, what number should we have said?
label: hard
why: No alarming keyword -- it looks like a contract-wording question -- so a matcher routes fast. Answering requires composing an end-to-end latency budget across four stages, reasoning about tail rather than median behavior, accounting for queue backlog after any stall and replica lag under write bursts, and then converting that into an honest defensible SLA number. Quantitative reasoning attached to a contractual commitment.

prompt: Draft the calendar invite description for our quarterly disaster recovery failover test: 90 minutes, next Wednesday, include a placeholder for the bridge link and a line asking attendees to read the runbook beforehand.
label: easy
why: Disaster recovery and failover test read as high-severity to a keyword matcher. The work is a short meeting blurb with every detail already provided by the requester. No system reasoning, no risk, and the output is three or four sentences of routine prose.

prompt: I need to port this function to the new event API. The old one guaranteed that callbacks fire in registration order; the new one's documentation says nothing about ordering at all. Existing callers depend on the old behavior even though it was never written down. What is the right way to do this?
label: hard
why: Ordinary refactoring vocabulary with no alarming term, so a matcher routes fast. The hard part is reasoning about an undocumented invariant that real callers depend on: whether to impose ordering in a shim, sort at dispatch, detect order-dependent callers, or accept the change with a migration path -- and weighing which choice silently breaks consumers. Preserving unspecified observable behavior across an API boundary is exactly the kind of subtle correctness work worth the deep tier.

prompt: In overflow_guard.c, rename the variable buf to frame_buffer everywhere it appears, including in the three comments that mention it by name. Do not change anything else.
label: easy
why: Overflow, guard, and buffer will trip a keyword matcher into escalating. The instruction is a scoped mechanical rename with an explicit do-not-touch-anything-else boundary. There is no design decision and no ambiguity; a fast model performs the substitution exactly as well as an expensive one.
